title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands, 2009
"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

The choropleth map displays the total mass of excess nitrogen from croplands for the year 2009, measured in tonnes. The color scale ranges from light blue for countries with nitrogen mining, where nutrient removal exceeds inputs, through pale yellow for low excess levels around 100,000 tonnes, to dark brown for the highest values reaching 25 million tonnes. The highest concentrations of excess nitrogen appear across major agricultural regions in Asia and North America. China and India occupy the darkest brown band, indicating values at or near the 25 million tonne maximum. The United States falls into the second-highest category, with excess nitrogen between 10 million and 25 million tonnes. Brazil and parts of Western Europe, including France and Germany, show values in the 1 million to 10 million tonne range. Australia and Canada display moderate levels in the 250,000 to 1 million tonne band. Large portions of sub-Saharan Africa, including the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Tanzania, along with Russia, appear in light blue, signifying nitrogen mining where soils lose more nutrients than they receive. The Middle East and North Africa show mixed patterns, with Egypt and Iran in the orange bands while surrounding nations remain in the yellow or blue categories. The geographic distribution places the greatest surplus loads in the world's most intensive cropping systems.
